  1869→                   additional_libs_list + 
  1870→		   test_link_args_base +

  1872→
  1873→      set_variable(test_inittls_linker_files_variable, [picolibc_ld_value])
  1874→
  1875→      # Make sure all of the tests get re-linked if the linker scripts change.
  1876→      set_variable(test_inittls_link_depends_variable, [picolibc_inittls_ld_value])
  1877→    endif
  1878→    # Prepare no-flash link args and depends using the noflash script
  1879→    if target == 'default-target'

  1886→    else
  1887→      picolibc_noflash_ld_string = '@0@'.format(picolibc_noflash_ld_value)
  1888→    endif
  1889→    set_variable(test_link_args_noflash_variable,
  1890→                 additional_libs_list +
  1891→                 test_link_args_base +
  1892→                 ['-nostdlib', '-T', picolibc_noflash_ld_string])
  1893→    set_variable(test_link_depends_noflash_variable, [picolibc_noflash_ld_value])
  1894→  endforeach
  1895→  test_noflash = true
  1896→else
  1897→  test_link_args = test_link_args_base

   119→process_command_line_argument ()

   158→    system)

   165→    c-compiler)
   166→        if [[ $OPTARG = "" ]]; then
   167→          abort "Option --c-compiler has an empty value.";
   168→        fi
   169→        C_COMPILER+=("$OPTARG")
   170→        ;;
   171→
   172→    linker)
   173→        if [[ $OPTARG = "" ]]; then
   174→          abort "Option --linker has an empty value.";
   175→        fi
   176→        LINKER+=("$OPTARG")
   177→        ;;
   178→
   179→    endianness)
   180→        if [[ $OPTARG = "" ]]; then
   181→          abort "Option --endianness has an empty value.";
   182→        fi
   183→        ENDIANNESS="$OPTARG"
   184→        ;;
   185→
   186→    cflag)
   187→        if [[ $OPTARG = "" ]]; then
   188→          abort "Option --cflag has an empty value.";
   189→        fi
   190→        CFLAGS_FOR_TARGET+=("$OPTARG")
   191→        ;;
   192→
   193→    lflag)
   194→        if [[ $OPTARG = "" ]]; then
   195→          abort "Option --lflag has an empty value.";
   196→        fi
   197→        LINKER_FLAGS_FOR_TARGET+=("$OPTARG")

   219→
   220→parse_command_line_arguments ()
   221→{
   222→  # The way command-line arguments are parsed below was originally described on the following page:
   223→  #   http://mywiki.wooledge.org/ComplexOptionParsing
   224→  # But over the years I have rewritten or amended most of the code myself.
   225→
   226→  if false; then
   227→    echo "USER_SHORT_OPTIONS_SPEC: $USER_SHORT_OPTIONS_SPEC"
   228→    echo "Contents of USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC:"
   229→    for key in "${!USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[@]}"; do
   230→      printf -- "- %s=%s\\n" "$key" "${USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[$key]}"
   231→    done
   232→  fi
   233→
   234→  # The first colon (':') means "use silent error reporting".
   235→  # The "-:" means an option can start with '-', which helps parse long options which start with "--".
   236→  local MY_OPT_SPEC=":-:$USER_SHORT_OPTIONS_SPEC"
   237→
   238→  local OPTION_NAME
   239→  local OPT_ARG_COUNT
   240→  local OPTARG  # This is a standard variable in Bash. Make it local just in case.
   241→  local OPTARG_AS_ARRAY
   242→
   243→  while getopts "$MY_OPT_SPEC" OPTION_NAME; do
   244→
   245→    case "$OPTION_NAME" in
   246→
   247→      -) # This case triggers for options beginning with a double hyphen ('--').
   248→         # If the user specified "--longOpt"   , OPTARG is then "longOpt".
   249→         # If the user specified "--longOpt=xx", OPTARG is then "longOpt=xx".
   250→
   251→         if [[ "$OPTARG" =~ .*=.* ]]  # With this --key=value format, only one argument is possible.
   252→         then
   253→
   254→           OPTION_NAME=${OPTARG/=*/}
   255→           OPTARG=${OPTARG#*=}
   256→           OPTARG_AS_ARRAY=("")
   257→
   258→           if ! test "${USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[$OPTION_NAME]+string_returned_if_exists}"; then
   259→             abort "Unknown command-line option \"--$OPTION_NAME\"."
   260→           fi
   261→
   262→           # Retrieve the number of arguments for this option.
   263→           OPT_ARG_COUNT=${USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[$OPTION_NAME]}
   264→
   265→           if (( OPT_ARG_COUNT != 1 )); then
   266→             abort "Command-line option \"--$OPTION_NAME\" does not take 1 argument."
   267→           fi
   268→
   269→           process_command_line_argument
   270→
   271→         else  # With this format, multiple arguments are possible, like in "--key value1 value2".
   272→
   273→           OPTION_NAME="$OPTARG"
   274→
   275→           if ! test "${USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[$OPTION_NAME]+string_returned_if_exists}"; then
   276→             abort "Unknown command-line option \"--$OPTION_NAME\"."
   277→           fi
   278→
   279→           # Retrieve the number of arguments for this option.
   280→           OPT_ARG_COUNT=${USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC[$OPTION_NAME]}
   281→
   282→           if (( OPT_ARG_COUNT == 0 )); then
   283→             OPTARG=""
   284→             OPTARG_AS_ARRAY=("")
   285→             process_command_line_argument
   286→           elif (( OPT_ARG_COUNT == 1 )); then
   287→             # If this is the last option, and its argument is missing, then OPTIND is out of bounds.
   288→             if (( OPTIND > $# )); then
   289→               abort "Option '--$OPTION_NAME' expects one argument, but it is missing."
   290→             fi
   291→             OPTARG="${!OPTIND}"
   292→             OPTARG_AS_ARRAY=("")
   293→             process_command_line_argument
   294→           else
   295→             OPTARG=""
   296→             # OPTARG_AS_ARRAY is not standard in Bash. I have introduced it to make it clear that
   297→             # arguments are passed as an array in this case. It also prevents many Shellcheck warnings.
   298→             OPTARG_AS_ARRAY=("${@:OPTIND:OPT_ARG_COUNT}")
   299→
   300→             if [ ${#OPTARG_AS_ARRAY[@]} -ne "$OPT_ARG_COUNT" ]; then
   301→               abort "Command-line option \"--$OPTION_NAME\" needs $OPT_ARG_COUNT arguments."
   302→             fi
   303→
   304→             process_command_line_argument
   305→           fi;
   306→
   307→           ((OPTIND+=OPT_ARG_COUNT))
   308→         fi
   309→         ;;
   310→
   311→      *) # This processes only single-letter options.
   312→         # getopts knows all valid single-letter command-line options, see USER_SHORT_OPTIONS_SPEC above.
   313→         # If it encounters an unknown one, it returns an option name of '?'.
   314→         if [[ "$OPTION_NAME" = "?" ]]; then
   315→           abort "Unknown command-line option \"$OPTARG\"."
   316→         else
   317→           # Process a valid single-letter option.
   318→           OPTARG_AS_ARRAY=("")
   319→           process_command_line_argument
   320→         fi
   321→         ;;
   322→    esac
   323→  done
   324→
   325→  shift $((OPTIND-1))
   326→  ARGS=("$@")
   327→}

   330→escape_for_meson_string ()
   331→{
   332→  local    STR="$1"
   333→  local -r RESULT_VAR_NAME="$2"
   334→
   335→  if [[ $STR =~ [[:cntrl:]] ]]; then
   336→    abort "The argument to escape for a meson string contains ASCII control characters."
   337→  fi
   338→
   339→  STR="${STR//\\/\\\\}"  # Replace \ with \\
   340→  STR="${STR//\'/\\\'}"  # Replace ' with \'
   341→
   342→  printf -v "$RESULT_VAR_NAME" "%s" "$STR"
   343→}

   346→# Examples of the 3 types of output:
   347→#
   348→# c_args = []
   349→#
   350→# c_args = [ '-O2' ]
   351→#
   352→# c_args = [
   353→#            '-g',
   354→#            '-O2'
   355→#          ]

   357→generate_setting_as_meson_array ()
   358→{
   359→  local SETTING_NAME="$1"
   360→  local -n ARRAY="$2"  # Create a reference to the array passed by name.
   361→
   362→  local -i ARRAY_LEN="${#ARRAY[@]}"
   363→
   364→  SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY="$SETTING_NAME = ["
   365→
   366→  if (( ARRAY_LEN == 0 )); then
   367→    SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+="]"$'\n'
   368→    return
   369→  fi
   370→
   371→  local ESCAPED_ARG
   372→
   373→  if (( ARRAY_LEN == 1 )); then
   374→    escape_for_meson_string "${ARRAY[0]}" "ESCAPED_ARG"
   375→    SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+=" '${ESCAPED_ARG}' ]"$'\n'
   376→    return
   377→  fi
   378→
   379→  SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+=$'\n'
   380→
   381→  local -i BASE_INDENTATION_LEN="$(( ${#SETTING_NAME} + 3 ))"
   382→  local BASE_INDENTATION
   383→  printf -v BASE_INDENTATION "%*s" "$BASE_INDENTATION_LEN" ""
   384→
   385→  local -r EXTRA_INDENTATION="  "
   386→
   387→  local -i INDEX
   388→
   389→  for ((INDEX=0; INDEX < ARRAY_LEN; INDEX++)); do
   390→
   391→    escape_for_meson_string "${ARRAY[$INDEX]}" "ESCAPED_ARG"
   392→
   393→    SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+="${BASE_INDENTATION}${EXTRA_INDENTATION}'$ESCAPED_ARG'"
   394→
   395→    if (( INDEX != ARRAY_LEN - 1 )); then
   396→      SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+=","
   397→    fi
   398→
   399→    SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+=$'\n'
   400→
   401→  done
   402→
   403→  SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY+="${BASE_INDENTATION}]"$'\n'
   404→}

   407→# If the command argument array is empty, no setting is generated.

   409→generate_command_setting ()
   410→{
   411→  local SETTING_NAME="$1"

   416→  local -i ARRAY_LEN="${#ARRAY[@]}"

   418→  if (( ARRAY_LEN == 0 )); then
   419→    COMMAND_SETTING=""
   420→    return

   422→
   423→  if (( ARRAY_LEN == 1 )); then
   424→
   425→    # We could generate an array with just one element, but a string looks nicer.
   426→    # Other sibling settings like 'ar' or 'strip' are usually given as strings too.
   427→
   428→    local ESCAPED_ARG
   429→    escape_for_meson_string "${ARRAY[0]}" "ESCAPED_ARG"
   430→
   431→    COMMAND_SETTING="$SETTING_NAME = '${ESCAPED_ARG}'"$'\n'
   432→    return
   433→  fi
   434→
   435→  local SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY
   436→
   437→  generate_setting_as_meson_array "$SETTING_NAME" "$ARRAY_NAME"
   438→
   439→  COMMAND_SETTING="$SETTING_AS_MESON_ARRAY"
   440→}

   442→
   443→# ----- Entry point -----
   444→
   445→USER_SHORT_OPTIONS_SPEC=""
   446→
   447→# Use an associative array to declare how many arguments every long option expects.
   448→# All known options must be listed, even those with 0 arguments.
   449→declare -A USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC
   450→USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC+=( [help]=0 )
   451→USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC+=( [version]=0 )
   452→USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC+=( [license]=0 )

   462→USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC+=( [exewrap]=1 )
   463→USER_LONG_OPTIONS_SPEC+=( [meson-compat]=1 )

   468→declare -a LINKER_FLAGS_FOR_TARGET=()
   469→declare -a EXE_WRAPPER=()

   487→fi

## Summary: QEMU Test Execution in picolibc_agentic

I've successfully mapped out the complete test infrastructure for how tests are run with QEMU in the picolibc_agentic project. Here's the full picture:

### 1. **Test Enable/Disable in Meson**

**File:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/meson_options.txt` (lines 86-105)

Key options:
- `tests` (boolean, default: false) - Master switch to enable tests
- `test-machine` (string, default: 'qemu') - Specifies which machine startup code to use (e.g., 'qemu', alternatives possible)
- `tests-enable-stack-protector` (boolean, default: true)
- Various other test-specific options like `test-stdin`, `split-large-tests`, etc.

**Usage:** 
```bash
meson setup -Dtests=true -Dtest-machine=qemu builddir
```

### 2. **Cross-File exe_wrapper Configuration**

The exe_wrapper is the key to QEMU execution in cross-compilation. It's set in meson cross-files:

**Pattern across all cross-files:**
```
exe_wrapper = ['env', 'run-<arch>']
```

**Examples:**

1. **RISC-V 64:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/cross-riscv64-unknown-elf.txt` (line 9)
   ```
   exe_wrapper = ['env', 'run-riscv']
   ```

2. **RISC-V 64 Zephyr:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/cross-riscv64-zephyr-elf.txt` (line 9)
   ```
   exe_wrapper = ['env', 'run-riscv']
   ```

3. **ARM:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/cross-arm-none-eabi.txt` (line 9)
   ```
   exe_wrapper = ['env', 'run-arm']
   ```

4. **AArch64:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/cross-aarch64-none-elf.txt` (line 9)
   ```
   exe_wrapper = ['env', 'run-aarch64']
   ```

### 3. **QEMU Wrapper Scripts**

**Location:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/run-*`

These are the actual QEMU launchers. The `run-riscv` script (5252 bytes) is most complex:

**Key features in `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/run-riscv`:**

- Auto-detects 32-bit vs 64-bit architecture using `readelf` on ELF files
- Parses `.riscv.attributes` section to determine required CPU extensions
- Dynamically selects `qemu-system-riscv32` or `qemu-system-riscv64`
- Configures QEMU CPU flags based on detected extensions
- Supports `--stdin` and `--args` parameters for tests
- Uses semihosting for I/O:
  ```
  -chardev stdio,mux=on,id=stdio0
  -semihosting-config enable=on,chardev=stdio0,arg="program-name $args"
  -machine virt,accel=tcg
  -nographic -bios none
  ```

### 4. **Meson Test Environment Setup**

**File:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/meson.build` (line 1804-1805)

```python
test_env = environment()
test_env.prepend('PATH', meson.current_source_dir() / 'scripts')
```

This ensures the `run-*` scripts in the `scripts/` directory are found when meson executes tests. The scripts rely on having `run-riscv` (et al.) in the PATH.

### 5. **Test Registration in Meson**

**File:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/test/meson.build` (lines 144-153 example)

Tests are registered with meson using the `test()` function:

```python
test(t1 + target,
     executable(t1 + target, ['try-ilp32.c', 'try-ilp32-sub.c', 'lock-valid.c'],
        c_args: _c_args,
        link_args: _link_args,
        objects: _objs,
        link_depends: _link_depends,
        include_directories: inc),
     depends: bios_bin,
     suite: 'test',
     env: test_env)
```

**How it works:**
1. Test executable is compiled with cross-compiler
2. When `meson test` is run, meson uses the `exe_wrapper` from the cross-file
3. The wrapper becomes: `env <PATH with scripts> run-riscv <test-exe>`
4. The `run-riscv` script invokes QEMU with proper semihosting configuration

### 6. **Cross-File Generation (Optional)**

**File:** `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/GeneratePicolibcCrossFile.sh`

This helper script can generate cross-files programmatically with the `--exewrap` option:
```bash
./GeneratePicolibcCrossFile.sh \
  --target-arch=riscv64-unknown-elf \
  --cpu-family=riscv64 \
  --exewrap='env' \
  --exewrap='run-riscv'
```

### 7. **Building & Running Tests**

**Typical workflow:**

```bash
# Configure with tests enabled
meson setup -Dtests=true -Dtest-machine=qemu \
  --cross-file scripts/cross-riscv64-unknown-elf.txt \
  builddir

# Run tests
meson test -C builddir
```

Meson will:
1. Compile all test executables using the cross-compiler
2. For each test, run: `scripts/run-riscv <exe>`
3. The run-riscv script invokes `qemu-system-riscv64` or `qemu-system-riscv32` with semihosting
4. QEMU captures output via semihosting chardev and returns exit code

### Key Files Summary

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/meson_options.txt` | Test configuration options |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/meson.build` (line 193, 1804-1805) | Test environment setup |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/test/meson.build` | Test registration |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/cross-*-elf.txt` | Cross-files with exe_wrapper |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/run-riscv` | RISCV QEMU wrapper (5252 bytes, complex) |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/run-arm` | ARM QEMU wrapper |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/run-aarch64` | AArch64 QEMU wrapper |
| `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/scripts/GeneratePicolibcCrossFile.sh` | Cross-file generator |

This architecture allows picolibc to run embedded system tests on the build host using QEMU, with proper semihosting for I/O capture and exit code handling.
